title: entries of texlab.symbols.allowedPatterns ?
author: malb
body:
Hi there,

Where would I find a list of things I can put into `texlab.symbols.allowedPatterns` and friends? Say, I want to suppress BibTeX keys, where would I look up what would need to go into `texlab.symbols.allowedPatterns` to faciliate that?

title: Please clarify LLM contributions
author: werdahias
body:
I was saddened to see a Co-Authored by Claude commit when preparing the new version for Debian. 
Please clarify how you intend to use LLM contributions in this project, because I won't be maintaining it it the long rung if any more such contributions happen.

title: Forward Search is broken on Tectonic projects
author: hethon
body:
Forward search does not work for Tectonic projects that are configured according to the wiki.

Given the following project:

```text
.
├── Tectonic.toml
└── src
    ├── _postamble.tex
    ├── _preamble.tex
    └── index.tex
```

and the recommended TexLab configuration:

```json
{
  "texlab.build.executable": "tectonic",
  "texlab.build.args": [
    "-X",
    "compile",
    "%f",
    "--synctex",
    "--keep-logs",
    "--keep-intermediates"
  ]
}
```

TexLab successfully builds the document. `tectonic -X compile` places the generated files alongside the source file:

```text
.
├── Tectonic.toml
└── src
    ├── _postamble.tex
    ├── _preamble.tex
    ├── index.aux
    ├── index.log
    ├── index.pdf
    ├── index.synctex.gz
    └── index.tex
```

However, invoking forward search fails with:

```text
ERROR - Failed to execute forward search: PDF document "/path/to/project/build/index.pdf" not found
```

The PDF exists at `src/index.pdf`, but TexLab appears to look for it in `build/`.

I initially expected `texlab.build.pdfDirectory` to control where TexLab looks for the generated PDF. However, changing this setting had no effect.

While investigating, I found that Tectonic projects appear to derive their output directory from a fixed `build/` path rather than from `texlab.build.pdfDirectory`:

https://github.com/latex-lsp/texlab/blob/adc91ff8a3b56ba4b72c489db33f32cfbb8b2c5c/crates/base-db/src/deps/root.rs#L61

## Current workaround

I configured Tectonic to emit outputs into `build/` using `--outdir build`.

While this makes forward search work, it requires the `build` directory to exist beforehand, as Tectonic does not create the output directory automatically.

---

## Proposed fix

For Tectonic projects, use `texlab.build.pdfDirectory` when resolving the output PDF instead of assuming a fixed output location.

```diff
-        let out_dir = dir.join("build/").unwrap();
+        let config = workspace.config();
+        let out_dir =
+            append_dir(dir, &config.build.pdf_dir, workspace).unwrap_or_else(|_| dir.clone());
```

This would allow all of the following workflows:

Using **`tectonic -X compile`**

```json
{
  "texlab.build.executable": "tectonic",
  "texlab.build.args": [
    "-X",
    "compile",
    "%f",
    "--synctex",
    "--keep-logs",
    "--keep-intermediates"
  ],
  "texlab.build.pdfDirectory": "src"
}
```

Using **`tectonic -X build`**

```json
{
  "texlab.build.executable": "tectonic",
  "texlab.build.args": [
    "-X",
    "build",
    "--keep-logs",
    "--keep-intermediates"
  ],
  "texlab.build.filename": "default.pdf",
  "texlab.build.pdfDirectory": "build/default"
}
```

(with `synctex = true` in `Tectonic.toml`).

Using `texlab.build.pdfDirectory` instead of assuming a fixed output directory would make forward search work with both `tectonic -X compile` and `tectonic -X build`, as well as custom output layouts.

---
## Backwards compatibility

Strictly speaking, this is fixing a broken feature rather than changing existing behavior, since forward search does not currently work with the documented Tectonic setup.

The only potentially affected users are those who configured Tectonic to output into `build/` as a workaround. If the implementation switches to using `texlab.build.pdfDirectory`, those users may need to set that option explicitly to preserve their current setup.

title: Document `latexFormatter = "none"`
author: mo8it
body: Currently, the code supports the value `"none"` for `latexFormatter` to disable formatting. But this option is not included in the wiki.

title: bug: `workspace/didChangeWatchedFiles` leaves stale build diagnostics after final LaTeX artifacts are updated
author: nghiant03
body:
### Description
After an async LaTeX build from a clean state, Texlab publishes first-pass undefined reference / undefined citation
diagnostics and does not fully clear them when the final `.log`, `.aux`, and `.bbl` files are updated via
`workspace/didChangeWatchedFiles`.

### Reproduction
  1. Initialize Texlab with a workspace rooted at a LaTeX project
  2. `textDocument/didOpen` `main.tex`
  3. Delete build artifacts (`main.aux`, `main.log`, `main.bbl`, etc.)
  4. Run `pdflatex` once
  5. Send `workspace/didChangeWatchedFiles` for `main.log` and `main.aux`
  6. Run `bibtex`, then `pdflatex`, then `pdflatex`
  7. Send `workspace/didChangeWatchedFiles` for final `main.log`, `main.aux`, and `main.bbl`

title: Memory leak when using Neovim v0.12 and texlab
author: DiegoCostaLopes
body:
Neovim is leaking memory when used with Texlab.

The memory leak happens only in insert mode. As soon as I start typing, RAM usage starts rising (some 50MB per line) and doesn't stop until I restart neovim.

Editing other filetypes doesn't cause the issue.

LSPs are configured in native neovim. Texlab is installed and working. If I disable texlab at lsp.lua and restart neovim, the memory leak stops (but I lose an important part of my workflow because the LSP grabs references from the .bib file, so disabling it doesn't help me much). Disabling the LSP with :lsp disable on the active buffer doesn't stop the leak immediately, but after typing some more it stops adding RAM usage.

I've created a log with by adding `  -- cmd = { "texlab", "-vvvv", "--log-file=/tmp/texlab.log" }, ` to my lspconfig. The file is attached.

[texlab.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26647950/texlab.log)

Since this is a problem that happens only with texlab but the memory leak is in neovim, I'm not even sure on which github repo should I post the issue. Any further help diagnosing the problem is appreciated. Thanks!

title: False undefined reference error with optional argument to bibitem
author: MithicSpirit

title: Unused entry in biblatex despite use via `\parencites`
author: SpookyYomo
body:
I was following <https://tex.stackexchange.com/a/488098> to utilise
`\parencites[...]{...}` instead of `\citep` or `\citet` from biblatex.
Consquently, texlab started reporting in my bib file that I was having
these `citet`->`parencites` citations as being unused entries.

Additional information:
- texlab v5.25.1
- nvim v0.11.6
- mason v2.2.1
- latexmk v4.87
- texlive-* v2026.0-3 (Archlinux)

title: Package Detection
author: janishutz
body:
Packages in `/usr/share/texmf/` are only suggested for `usepackage` Arch Linux (this location is referenced by $TEXMFLOCAL by default) and no commands from in them are autocompleted (this affects my own package I am currently writing and others from the AUR).
The detection for `\newcommand`, etc (to some extent) is clearly there, as if imported as `.tex` file, all commands are suggested, however, not if using usepackage (only for default LaTeX packages)

How does texlab get the completion content and why does it not work for these files?

(I am using the most recent version through nvim-lspconfig)

title: BibTeX: citations not found for dynamically constructed \input{} paths
author: l-zeuch
body:
In my `main.tex`, I have the following definiton:

```tex
\addbibresource{K4/k4.bib}

\def\ex{K4}
% [snip]
\edef\exl{\makelower{\ex}}

\input{\ex/\exl.tex} % this expands to K4/k4.tex and compiles
```
A `k4.bib` file is placed directly next to `k4.tex`, i.e. inside the `K4/` directory.

However, it seems like `texlab` cannot handle this -- admittedly weird construction -- complaining about undefined references (in `*.tex`) *and* unused entries (in `*.bib`). Worth noting that everything compiles just fine, including the references. It's just the LSP throwing me off-guard.

Changing it to `\input{K4/k4.tex}` yields the expected result: citation keys are found and only the actually unused entries are marked as such.

For context: I would definitely agree that you can consider this not best practise; however, it is not an error and therefore the LSP should not throw errors either. It can *inform* about potential issues with this "dynamic" input file finding or something akin to that. I also tested this on Overleaf to see how they handle citation key discovery. There, they find the keys as expected.

----

My reasoning for this absurd™️ file discovery is that I am sharing this as a template for future students in a chemistry lab; while most of them definitely know their way around LaTeX, I don't want them having to change a bunch of lines when working on a new report. Similarly, they're likely to collaborate on Overleaf, so I should make sure it works there.

Edit: In case I accidentally elided crucial information, you can view the "template" here: <https://github.com/l-zeuch/phys-chem-reports>

title: Chinese characters are not shown properly in file paths in the coc#status information
author: zhiyuanzhai
body: I am using TexLab in neovim through coc.nvim. The problem lies with the Chinese characters in the building message, which is followed by the current working file path. The problem was previously reported in https://github.com/fannheyward/coc-texlab/issues/258, but I recently found out that it actually has nothing to do with that extension. It does not handle those messages. By the way, is there an option not to show the absolute path of the building file, only showing the relative path to the pwd, or just showing nothing instead?

title: Way to disable picture completion for symbols.
author: PhyX-Meow
body: Hi, I'm using vim lsp, which does not support display png pictures. I see there is an option to let texlab not provide png pictures when hover. Could we also have an option to let it do not send picture when provide completion? Many thanks.

title: Support for Tectonic v2 workspaces
author: wvffle
body:
Hi, I'm used to write my LaTeX documents as Tectonic workspace created with `tectonic -X init`. To build such project, I use `tectonic -X build` rather than `tectonic -X compile`.

By default it builds as `./build/default/default.pdf` file, however texlab hardcodes all directories for Tectonic projects to `./build/` in:
https://github.com/latex-lsp/texlab/blob/d89bf0fa65bd931ab57183d067f66189fc31e6c9/crates/base-db/src/deps/root.rs#L61-L64

Hence, I need to set the `build.filename` to `default/default.pdf` for texlab to correctly detect the PDF file. 

But for synctex to work properly, I need to also set the `build.pdfDirectory` to `build/default` and in `forwardSearch.args` pass `build/default/default.pdf` rather  than `%p`. 

I cannot get any compilation errors to show up in my editor and I need to check the log file for that. I could only get building and synctex to work properly. However it feels very hacky.

Below you have my current configuration. 
```lua
      {
        build = {
          -- Tectonic project configuration
          -- NOTE: the `pdfDirectory` option is ignored and a hardcoded value of `build` is used, when `Tectonic.toml` is detected,
          --       however I need to set the `pdfDirectory` to `build/default`, otherwise synctex does not work properly.
          pdfDirectory = "build/default",
          filename = "default/default.pdf",

          executable = "tectonic",
          args = {
            "-X",
            "build",
            "--keep-logs",
            "--keep-intermediates",
          },

          -- NOTE: Those values are also hardcoded to `build`, no compile error detection unfortunately :(
          auxDirectory = "build/default",
          logDirectory = "build/default",

          -- Build on save and do forward search afterward.
          onSave = true,
          forwardSearchAfter = true,
        },

        -- Run zathura when we want to forward search.
        forwardSearch = {
          executable = "zathura",
          args = {
            "--synctex-forward=%l:1:%f",
            -- NOTE: For tectonic projects, we need to specify the PDF path rather than using `%p` magic value, as it contains
            --       wrong path. See NOTE comment above.
            "build/default/default.pdf",
          },
        },
      }
```

title: Texlab crashes sometimes when using it with nvim
author: tmke8
body:
I see this in the `lsp.log` file:


```
[ERROR][2025-11-10 23:12:57] ...p/_transport.lua:36     "rpc"   "texlab"        "stderr"        "\nthread 'main' panicked at crates
/texlab/src/server.rs:396:77:\ncalled `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value\nnote: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable
to display a backtrace\n"
```

It seems to come from this line:

https://github.com/latex-lsp/texlab/blob/28e952339fcea0e7e2deea09f5146f5622a19d1a/crates/texlab/src/server.rs#L396

I don't really know what exactly triggers it. From my perspective it seems to happen randomly. If I can do something to debug this further, let me know.

title: Feature Request: Outline for beamer frames
author: msrd0
body: I am using texlab with Code-OSS and am currently working on a LaTeX Beamer document. I noticed that the outline shows a lot of itemize environments, but not the frame environments around them. It would be super helpful if those were included, ideally together with the frame title, in the outline.

title: tex-fmt formatter does not respect "Formatter Line Length" setting
author: inklesspen
body:
The default Formatter Line Length value is 80, and tex-fmt's default line-wrapping column is 80, so this is easy to miss.

But if Formatter Line Length is set to a different value, you need to add `--wraplen` to the arguments.
